questions.prompt: A customer has one office with both a wired LAN (192.168.10.0/24) and Wi-Fi (192.168.20.0/24). The two segments can route to each other and to the printers. What's the cleanest design?
choices: Two Sites, one for the LAN and one for Wi-Fi
One Site with two Networks, one per gateway
One Site with one Network spanning both gateways
Skip Sites entirely and use only the default
questions.explanation: Sites group Networks logically, and a Network is identified by gateway IP and MAC. Two distinct gateways means two Networks; one office means one Site. Splitting into two Sites duplicates Site manager and group attachments unnecessarily.

questions.prompt: Customers with zero-trust networks can use Printix without any specific configuration; the Printix Client figures it out automatically.
questions.explanation: Zero-trust networks need explicit configuration so the Printix Client can determine its Site. The Implementation checklist asks 'Zero trust networks: Yes/No' as a planning question, and there are documented zero-trust setup steps.

questions.prompt: If no model-specific print driver is available in the customer's driver store for a printer, what does Printix do by default?
choices: Refuses to add the queue and emits an error
Falls back to a vendor universal driver from the supported OEM list (HP, Canon, Konica Minolta, Kyocera, Lexmark, Ricoh, and others)
Generates a synthetic driver from the printer's MIB tree
Uses Microsoft's basic IPP class driver
questions.explanation: Printix maintains a set of vendor universal drivers as fallbacks when no model-specific driver exists. Check the Drivers page in Administrator for the live list in a given tenant rather than memorising a fixed enumeration.

questions.prompt: Printix's documented print-server decommissioning advice is to take the server fully offline immediately once printers are registered in Printix.
questions.explanation: The recommended path is unplug-but-don't-decommission for about a week, watch for complaints, and only fully decommission if nobody surfaces a problem.

questions.prompt: Enabling Microsoft Entra group synchronisation in Printix requires the consenting account to hold the Global Administrator role.
questions.explanation: Documented Printix requirement. A User Administrator or other lower role hits the 'Could not verify group synchronization' error.

questions.prompt: A customer's Microsoft Entra group 'All Sydney' contains two child groups, 'Sydney Reception' and 'Sydney Sales'. The MSP attaches 'All Sydney' to a print queue's Exclusive access. Which users can use the queue?
choices: All members of 'Sydney Reception' and 'Sydney Sales', via the nested membership
Direct members of 'All Sydney' only; nested groups are not supported
All users in Microsoft Entra ID; nesting widens the scope
Nobody, because Printix rejects groups containing other groups
questions.explanation: Printix doesn't traverse nested groups. Only direct members of 'All Sydney' get access. Customers either flatten the nesting or attach the leaf groups directly.

questions.prompt: Which of these are valid uses of Microsoft Entra groups attached to print queues in Printix? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Add print queue automatically when a member signs in
Exclusive access to limit who can use the queue
Site manager group for delegated administration
Workflow access for a Capture workflow
Configuring Microsoft Entra Conditional Access policies
questions.explanation: All four (auto-add, exclusive access, site manager, workflow) are documented Printix uses of groups. Conditional Access is a Microsoft Entra concept, not a Printix one.

questions.prompt: Local IT at a customer's branch office wants to add a printer when one breaks, without raising a ticket with the MSP. What's the documented Printix way to enable that?
choices: Share the System manager account credentials
Create a Microsoft Entra group for the local IT, and attach it as a Site manager group on that branch's Site
Give them Owner role on the tenant
Have them register a separate Printix Home for that branch
questions.explanation: The Site manager role exists for exactly this scope. The local IT signs in with their own account, sees only their Site, and can add / modify / delete printers and queues on that Site.

questions.prompt: Which of these can a Site manager NOT do? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Modify the customer's Authentication settings
Manage the customer's Subscription / billing
See or change other Sites
Discover printers on networks belonging to their managed sites
Modify the tenant-wide Drivers store
questions.explanation: Site managers can do everything inside their managed Sites (including discover-printers there) but cannot touch tenant-wide Authentication, Subscription, or the Drivers store, and cannot see or change other Sites.

questions.prompt: Which of these are statistics shown on the Administrator Dashboard? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Print activity (pages over the period)
Save-O-Meter (uncollected percentage)
Printed in black (mono ratio)
Printed 2-sided (duplex ratio)
Average queue wait time per printer
questions.explanation: The Dashboard surfaces those four counters plus Secure print percentage. Average queue wait time isn't a Printix Dashboard statistic.

questions.prompt: A Power BI Pro licence is required to publish the Printix Power BI report to the Power BI online service for shared access.
questions.explanation: Power BI Desktop alone (free) is enough to use the report on one workstation. Publishing to Power BI service for shared / web access requires Pro.

questions.prompt: The cost figures in the Printix Power BI report are a finance-grade ledger that should be used directly for invoicing customers.
questions.explanation: Printix's own documentation calls it an estimate based on configurable per-page costs. It's good for chargeback and trend-watching, not for tax-grade finance.
